summary: Gruber analyzes Avie Tevanian's tenure as Apple's software chief following his transition to a new role, framing it as a mixed legacy. On the positive side, Tevanian delivered remarkable project management: Mac OS X shipped and improved on a steady annual schedule, while simultaneously maintaining momentum on classic Mac OS 8-9. However, Gruber argues Tevanian's deep loyalty to NeXT technology over Apple's own led to serious usability failures, including the infamous Technote #2034 and the iTunes 2.0 installer disaster. The piece concludes by hoping his successor Bertrand Serlet will prioritize pragmatism and usability over technical dogma.
key_points: Tevanian's greatest achievement was establishing a reliable annual release schedule for Mac OS X, contrasting sharply with Microsoft's multi-year gaps between Windows releases.
Under Tevanian, Apple successfully maintained two major operating systems simultaneously (classic Mac OS 8-9 and Mac OS X), a significant management feat.
Tevanian's dogmatic preference for NeXT technologies over Apple's own led to concrete usability disasters, including the iTunes 2.0 installer bug that deleted entire volumes.
The withdrawn Technote #2034, reportedly written by Tevanian personally, exemplified his tin ear for Mac developer culture by mandating NeXT conventions over established Mac practices.
The choice of Mach kernel over Apple's NuKernel was made without apparent consideration of NuKernel's advantages, reflecting Tevanian's personal attachment to technology he co-created.
money_quotes: But where he's gone wrong, he's gone terribly wrong, flashing a spiteful streak that has been largely detrimental to Mac OS X's overall usability.
His technical acumen may be undisputed, but neither is his tin ear for usability.
The general perception is that Mac OS X isn't just good, but that it's good and getting better.
Even if Longhorn does ship in 2005, Apple will likely have shipped two major updates after Panther in that same time.
All too often, Tevanian's decisions and edicts have been dogmatic rather than pragmatic.
Mac developers tend to be independent thinkers, and flatly rejected the precepts of Tevanian's 'Guidelines'.
Many of Tevanian's design decisions have not been made with the best interests of Mac users in mind.
key_insight: Tevanian's tenure at Apple exemplifies how brilliant technical management and reliable execution can be undermined by dogmatic loyalty to one's own technology at the expense of usability.
